book_claims.claim: 40-60% of calcium is absorbed (higher bioavailability than cow's milk at 31-32%)
book_claims.mechanism: Low oxalate content allows high calcium bioavailability; oxalates in other vegetables (spinach, chard, beets) chelate calcium and block absorption

supplementary_research.finding: Calcium bioavailability from low-oxalate Brassica vegetables (broccoli, bok choy, kale) ranges from 49-61%, exceeding milk's 32%. Confirms the book's claim about superior calcium absorption.
Cruciferous vegetable intake inversely associated with all-cause mortality in a large Chinese cohort. Each additional daily serving linked to 10% reduction in total mortality.
Broccoli is among the top 20 foods by aggregate nutrient density index (ANDI). Steaming preserves 80-90% of sulforaphane content while boiling loses over 50%.
Sulforaphane from broccoli family vegetables is the most potent natural Nrf2 activator known, with human trials supporting anti-cancer, anti-diabetic, and neuroprotective effects.

culinary_pairings.source: Myrosinase in mustard seeds restores sulforaphane conversion after cooking
Fat enhances absorption of fat-soluble sulforaphane and vitamin K
Complementary organosulfur compounds; classic sauteed greens base
Acid brightens brassica flavors and adds vitamin C
Heat cuts through brassica sweetness; broccoli with garlic and chili is a staple
Toasted sesame complements nutty roasted brassica flavors
Classic Tuscan combination; complementary amino acid profiles

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: 49-61% bioavailability due to low oxalate content. Gram-for-gram, more calcium reaches the bloodstream than from milk (32% bioavailability).
Requires myrosinase activation. Steam max 3 minutes. Adding mustard powder after cooking restores conversion.
Fat-soluble. Essential for calcium metabolism and bone health. Pair with olive oil.
High bioavailability. Rapidly destroyed by prolonged cooking.
Potent inhibitor of WWP1, freeing tumor suppressor PTEN. Formed on mechanical disruption of plant cells.

synergies.description: Unlike spinach/chard/beets which are high in oxalates, cabbage/broccoli have low oxalates allowing better calcium absorption
